1.
The metal in an Australian 20 cent coin is one-quarter nickel, and
the rest is copper. What fraction is copper?
(A) 3
4
(B) 1
2
(C) 1
4
(D) 4
4
(E) 0
The fraction that is copper is 1 −1
4 = 3
4,
hence (A).

2.
Owen set the temperature dial on his oven as
shown. What temperature is it set to?
(A) 175 ◦C
(B) 185 ◦C
(C) 195 ◦C
(D) 205 ◦C
(E) 215 ◦C
OFF
100
150
200
250
◦C
Between 150 and 200 there are 5 intervals, so each interval represents 10 ◦C. The dial is
31
2 intervals above 150 ◦C which is therefore 185 ◦C,
hence (B).

3.
If the day before yesterday was Monday, then the day after tomorrow will be
(A) Monday
(B) Tuesday
(C) Wednesday
(D) Thursday
(E) Friday
If the day before yesterday was Monday, then yesterday was Tuesday and today must be
Wednesday. So tomorrow is Thursday and the day after tomorrow is Friday,
hence (E).

5.

(A) 40
(B) 50
(C) 60
(D) 70
(E) 80
80◦

Alternative 1
The angles in a quadrilateral add to 360◦, so x = 360 −90 −80 −120 = 70,
hence (D).
Alternative 2

the 20◦angle shown.
80◦
100◦

x◦
Finally x◦= 180◦−90◦−20◦= 70◦,
hence (D).

length h?

(B) 4 m

(E) 16 m
9 m

2bh,

h = 8
hence (D).

7.
What is the difference between 3 × (4 + 2) and (3 × 4) + 2?
(A) 0
(B) 2
(C) 4
(D) 6
(E) 8
Evaluating, 3 × (4 + 2) = 3 × 6 = 18 and (3 × 4) + 2 = 12 + 2 = 14. Then 18 −14 = 4,
hence (C).

8.
What number must be divided by 100 to get 0.2?
(A) 0.002
(B) 0.02
(C) 0.2
(D) 2
(E) 20
Let x be the number. Then x ÷ 100 = 0.2 so x = 100 × 0.2 = 20,
hence (E).

9.
The average of 2024 and an unknown number is 21
2. What is the unknown number?
(A) 2019
(B) −2019
(C) 2029
(D) −2029
(E) −2024
The two numbers add to 2 × (21
2) = 5 so the unknown number is 5 −2024 = −2019,
hence (B).

10. The Balga is a grass tree found in south-western Australia.
Balgas grow between 1 and 2 centimetres taller each year.
Approximately how old is a Balga that is 4 metres tall?
(A) 10 years
(B) 30 years
(C) 60 years
(D) 300 years
(E) 1000 years
Each 100 years, a Balga will grow between 1 and 2 metres. To reach 4 metres will take
between 4 centuries, at 1 metre per century, or 2 centuries, at 2 metres per century. Of
the answers given, 300 years is the best approximation,
hence (D).
Questions 11 to 20, 4 marks each
11-20 题，每题4 分

11. Danilo’s pasta recipe requires one 60-gram egg for each 100 grams of flour, and no other
ingredients. To make 800 grams of pasta, how many eggs does he need?
(A) 5
(B) 6
(C) 7
(D) 8
(E) 9
Suppose that one serve of pasta requires one 60 gram egg and 100 grams of flour, which
is a total of 160 grams of pasta. Then 800 grams of pasta amounts to 800 ÷ 160 = 5
serves. Since each serve requires one egg, 5 eggs are needed,
hence (A).

(D) 10
(E) 11
Alternative 1
Counting grid squares, the area is 2 × 3 + 1 × 3 = 9,
hence (C).
Alternative 2

1

2 × 3 × 1 = 3
2. The total shaded area is 6 + 2 × 3
2 = 9,
hence (C).

isosceles triangles as shown.
What is the value, in degrees, of ∠ABC?
(A) 50
(B) 55
(C) 60
(D) 65
(E) 70
|

80◦
Alternative 1
The unlabelled angles in the lower isosceles triangle are each 180 −80
2

obtuse angles in the upper two triangles are each 360 −80
2

angles of these two triangles are each 180 −140
2
= 20 degrees. Combining these,
∠ABC = 50 + 20 = 70 degrees,
hence (E).
Alternative 2
As in Alternative 1, the acute angles of the upper two isosceles triangles are each 20◦. So
∠BAC = 20◦+ 20◦= 40◦. Angles ABC and ACB are both 180 −40
2
= 70◦,
hence (E).

14. We have two water tanks for bushfire emergencies. The 10 kL
tank is 80% full and the 40 kL tank is 30% full. Regulations
state that we must have at least 30 kL available.
How much more water do we need to add to our tanks?
10 kL
40 kL
(A) 0 kL
(B) 5 kL
(C) 10 kL
(D) 15 kL
(E) 20 kL
The amount of water currently stored is 80
100 × 10 + 30
100 × 40 = 8 + 12 = 20 kilolitres. So
another 10 kL is required to make 30 kL,
hence (C).

15. I have a pad of 100 sticky notes. Each note is a
75 mm × 75 mm square.
Using most of the pad, I completely cover all the faces of a
cube. None of the notes folds over an edge of the cube.
What is the largest the side length of the cube could be?
(A) 22.5 cm
(B) 30 cm
(C) 45 cm
(D) 60 cm
(E) 75 cm
STICK-EE NOTES
100 self-stick notes
yellow 75 mm × 75 mm
Since 100 ÷ 6 is between 16 and 17, one possibility is to place 16 sticky notes on every
face in a 4 × 4 grid of notes. This can cover a cube of side length 300 mm = 30 cm.
No more is possible, since at least one face will have 16 or fewer sticky notes, and these
can, at most, exactly cover one face of a 300 mm cube,
hence (B).

16. In this square, all rows and columns have the same sum.
Find the average of A, B, C and D.
(A) 7
(B) 8
(C) 9
(D) 10
(E) 11
D
7
C
X
5
Y
A
11
B
The second column adds to 11 + 5 + 7 = 23 so this is the sum of each row and each
column. Then A + 11 + B = 23 so that A + B = 12. Similarly, X + Y = 18 and
C + D = 16. (For instance, A = B = 6, C = D = 8 and X = Y = 9 is one way to
complete the grid.) Then the average of A, B, C and D is
(A + B + C + D) ÷ 4 = 28 ÷ 4 = 7,
hence (A).

17. In this puzzle, the numbers from 1 to 8 are to be placed
in the 8 circles. For each of the 3 squares, the sum of
the 4 numbers on the corners must be 20.
With 5 and 7 placed as in the diagram, what number
goes in the middle circle?
(A) 1
(B) 2
(C) 3
(D) 4
(E) 6
5
?
7
Alternative 1
In the bottom square, we know 5 + 7 = 12 so the other two numbers add to 20 −12 = 8.
Since 5 and 7 are already used, these two numbers must be 2 and 6, and there are two
cases — the middle number is either 2 or 6.
If the middle number was 2, then the right square has 2 + 5 = 7 so the other two
numbers add to 20 −7 = 13. However, 13 can only be made as 5 + 8 or 6 + 7 and 5 and 7
are already used. So the middle number can’t be 2.
2
5
9
7
6
1
4
3
If the middle number is 6, the bottom number is 2, and we can
place 3 and 4 in the top left and 1 and 9 in the top right. This is the
only solution, so the middle number must be 6. One possibility for the
complete puzzle is shown,
hence (E).
Alternative 2
Try placing 1 in the middle, so that 2 + 3 + 4 + 6 + 8 = 23 is unused. However, we have
targets of 12 on the left, 14 on the right and 7 at the bottom, for a total of 33.
With 2 in the middle, 1+3+4+6+8 = 22 is unused and the targets are 11+13+6 = 30.
Every extra 1 in the middle decreases the unused total by 1 and decreases the total target
by 3. This narrows the gap by 2. They are both equal to 18 when the middle number is
6. With 6 in the middle, the rest of the grid is easily filled in, as in the earlier solution,
hence (E).
Alternative 3
The 8 numbers add to 36, but the 3 squares add to 60. The extra 24 is from counting the
numbers 5 and 7 a second time and from counting the middle number 3 times. So twice
the middle number is 24 −5 −7 = 12 and the middle number is 6,
hence (E).

18. A regular hexagon made from yellow cardboard has a thick black
stripe painted across it. The hexagon is cut up into six equilateral
triangles and the pieces are mixed up. This diagram shows one of
the pieces in its original position.
The other five pieces are shown in options (A) to (E).
Which piece was in the position marked with the X?
X
(A)
(B)
(C)
(D)
(E)
Alternative 1
Continuing the top edge of the black stripe to the right as shown in the first diagram, the
top two pieces must be (E) and (B). Only piece (D) has a small yellow corner, which
matches the top of the stripe as shown in the second diagram. The third diagram shows
where the remaining pieces fit.
B
E
X
B
E

X
B
E
A
C
D
,
hence (C).
Alternative 2
The lower-left triangle has a fully black edge, so must be (A). The lower-right triangle
has an edge that is slightly less than half black, which can only be (C). So triangle (C)
goes in position X,
hence (C).

19. A three-digit number has all digits nonzero. These digits are multiplied together and
then this product is subtracted from the original number. What is the largest possible
value of this difference?
(A) 890
(B) 901
(C) 909
(D) 910
(E) 990
Let N be the three-digit number, P the product of its digits and D = N −P. Examples
like N = 911 show that the biggest possible D is between 900 and 999, so both D and N
will begin with 9. Write N = 900 + 10b + c where b and c are nonzero digits. Then
P = 9bc and D = 900 + 10b + c −9bc = 900 + 10b −c(9b −1).
For any nonzero b, 9b −1 > 0 so that D is greatest when c = 1. Then D = 901 + b which
has maximum value 910 when b = 9 and n = 991,
hence (D).

20. A piece of wire of length 50 cm is bent into the shape shown, where each bend in the wire
forms a right angle. The three internal sections of the bent wire are each the same
length, x cm. What is the value of x?
26 cm
x
x
x
(A) 4
(B) 6
(C) 8
(D) 10
(E) 12
Alternative 1
The length of the sum of the two end sections of the bent wire is 26 + x because the
bottom section starts x back from the top section. Hence the total length of the bent
wire is 26 + 4x = 50 giving 4x = 24 so x = 6,
hence (B).
Alternative 2
Bend the wire further as indicated by the dotted lines.
x
x
x
26 cm
The doubled-up part of the wire is 50 −26 = 24 cm, so that x = 24 ÷ 4 = 6 cm,
hence (B).
Questions 21 to 25, 5 marks each
21-25 题，每题5 分

21. Amisha is wrapping a present for her little brother. The present is in a box measuring
9 cm × 9 cm × 13 cm and the sheet of wrapping paper is 40 cm × 25 cm. She wraps the
present as tightly as possible, as shown.
25
40
13
9
9
What percentage of the wrapping paper is no longer visible from any angle?
(A) 25%
(B) 30%
(C) 33%
(D) 37%
(E) 42%
The area of wrapping paper that is visible from the outside is the same as the total area
of the six faces of the box. This is the total surface area of the box. There are two square
faces with area 9 × 9 = 81 cm2 and four rectangular faces with area 9 × 13 = 117 cm2. So
this total area is
2 × 81 + 4 × 117 = 630 cm2
The full area of the wrapping paper is 40 × 25 = 1000 cm2. Therefore the percentage of
wrapping paper which is visible is
630
1000 × 100% = 63%
The remaining 37% is not visible,
hence (D).

shown below.
Xavier, Yasmin and Zebedee take turns removing balls from the chute. When a ball is

Xavier always takes the second available ball.
Yasmin always takes the third available ball.
Zebedee always takes the first available ball.
Which statement is true?

23. To make the stamps easier to pull apart, sheets of postage
stamps have rows of holes punched into them.
In the sheet shown, each stamp has 7 holes along the top and
bottom edges, 9 holes along the sides and 1 more at each corner.
Holes are included around all four edges of the sheet so the
borders can be torn off as well.
The full sheet has 20 stamps arranged in 4 rows of 5. How many
holes are there altogether?
(A) 320
(B) 391
(C) 421
(D) 451
(E) 640
60c
60c
AUSTRALIA
AUSTRALIA
60c
60c
60
60
AUSTRAL
AUSTRAL
60
60
Alternative 1
Break up the perforations for each stamp into four parts as shown on the left: the yellow
hook shape of 7 + 1 + 9 = 17 holes, the blue base strip of 1 + 7 = 8 holes, the pink side
strip of 1 + 9 = 10 holes and the green corner with 1 hole.
60c
60c
AUSTRALIA
AUSTRALIA
To avoid double counting overlapping edges, the entire sheet can be accounted for by the
arrangement shown on the right: 20 yellow hooks (one per stamp), 5 blue base strips, 4
pink side strips and 1 green corner.
So the total number of holes is
(20 × 17) + (5 × 8) + (4 × 10) + 1 = 421,
hence (C).
Alternative 2
There are 6 columns of holes and 5 rows of holes, each with 41 holes. Counting this as
41 × 11 = 451, we have double-counted 6 × 5 = 30 corner holes that are in both a row and
a column. Subtracting to compensate for double-counting, there are 451 −30 = 421 holes,
hence (C).

24. Sally arranges the numbers from 1 to 8 in a circle and then calculates the sum of each set
of 3 adjacent numbers. She would like an arrangement in which the smallest of these
sums is as large as possible. If she chooses such an arrangement, what will this smallest
sum be?
(A) 10
(B) 11
(C) 12
(D) 13
(E) 14
Let the smallest of the sums of three adjacent numbers be x.
1
7
4
3
8
2
5
6
14
12
14
15
13
15
13
12
Then adding from the number 1 around the circle and back to
1 adds 9 numbers in a row, so this value is at least 3x. However
these 9 numbers add to 1 + 1 + 2 + 3 + · · · + 8 = 37. Hence
37 ⩾3x so that 121
3 ⩾x and since x is a whole number, x ⩽12.
Once it is known that 12 has a chance of being the largest mini-
mum, it is straightforward to find one of the many arrangements
where all sums of 3 adjacent numbers (shown in blue) are 12 or
more, such as the arrangement shown. This confirms that the
smallest possible sum of 3 adjacent numbers is 12,
hence (C).

25. A totally even number is a positive integer in which all the digits are even. The list of all
totally even numbers begins as follows:
2, 4, 6, 8, 20, 22, 24, 26, 28, 40, . . .
The number 40 is in the 10th position in this list. In which position is the totally even
number 2024?
(A) 57
(B) 132
(C) 257
(D) 512
(E) 1012
The units digit cycles though the 5 even digits: 2, 4, 6, 8, 0, 2, 4, 6, . . .. To make the cycle
clearer, temporarily start with the number 0, so that the cycles finishes after the 5th,
10th, . . . position, and the tens digit increases there as well.
Every 5 positions, the tens digit increases in the same cycle of even digits, taking 25
positions to cycle. Likewise the hundreds digit increases every 25 positions, for a cycle of
125 positions and the thousands digit increases every 125 positions. So the list up to
2024 is
0, 2, 4, . . . , 888
|
{z
}
125
, 2000, 2002, . . . , 2008
|
{z
}
5
, 2020, 2022, 2024
|
{z
}
3
which has 2024 in position 133. Without the initial 0, 2024 is in position 132,
hence (B).
For questions 26 to 30, shade the answer as an integer from 0 to 999
in the space provided on the answer sheet.
问题26-30 的答案为0-999 之间的整数，请将答案填涂在答题卡对应区域。
Questions 26–30 are worth 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10 marks, respectively.
第26-30 题分别为6, 7, 8, 9, 10 分。

26. Ringo has 5 cards with the numbers 2, 3, 4, 5 and
6 on them. He wrote a list of all possible two-digit
numbers that can be made with these cards.
Find the sum of all numbers in his list that are
not prime.
2
3
4
5
6
Alternative 1
We first consider the sum of all possible numbers, including primes. The numbers in the
list are
23, 24, 25, 26, 32, 34, 35, 36, 42, 43, 45, 46, 52, 53, 54, 56, 62, 63, 64, 65
Each digit occurs in the units position 4 times, so the units digits add to
4 × (2 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 6) = 80. Similarly the tens digits add to 80, so that the total of all 20
numbers is 80 + 800 = 880.
The only primes in the list are 23, 43 and 53, which add to 119. So the required sum is
880 −119 = 761,
hence (761).
Alternative 2
There are 5 × 4 = 20 choices of tens digit then ones digit. These 20 numbers can be
arranged into 10 pairs, each adding to 88:
88 = 23 + 65 = 24 + 64 = · · ·
These 10 pairs add to 10 × 88 = 880.
As in Alternative 1, subtracting the three primes gives 880 −119 = 761,
hence (761).

27. A farmer, Jenni, grows oranges and grapefruit. At her last harvest she picked 120
grapefruit and a number of oranges. However, one-tenth of all grapefruit look like
oranges and one-tenth of all oranges look like grapefruit. Overall, one-fifth of all fruits
look like grapefruit.
How many oranges did Jenni harvest?
Of the 120 grapefruit she picks, 12 look like oranges, so 108 look like grapefruit.
Suppose Jenni picks 10x oranges. Then 9x look like oranges and x look like grapefruit.
Of the 120 + 10x fruit picked, one fifth look like grapefruit, which is 24 + 2x. This is also
equal to 108 + x. Solving,
24 + 2x = 108 + x
x = 108 −24 = 84
So the number of oranges picked is 10x = 840,
hence (840).

28. A rectangular piece of paper would lose 999 cm2 of area if a 3 cm wide border was
trimmed from all four sides. If instead the trimmed border was 2 cm wide, what area, in
square centimetres, would be lost?
Alternative 1
Let the dimensions of the rectangle be x cm × y cm. When the 3 cm border is trimmed,
the width and height both reduce by 6 cm. So the area lost, in square centimetres, is
xy −(x −6)(y −6) = xy −(xy −6x −6y + 36) = 6(x + y) −36.
Setting this area equal to 999 and solving shows that x + y = 1035
6
= 345
2 .
If the border is 2 cm wide instead, the width and height both reduce by 2 cm. So the area
lost, in square centimetres, is
xy −(x −4)(y −4) = 4(x + y) −16 = 4 × 345
2 −16 = 2 × 345 −16 = 674
hence (674).
Alternative 2
Trim the border 1 cm at a time, creating a sequence of border trimmings. Each can be
made up of 4 rectangles, each 1 cm wide, and each rectangle loses 2 cm length from one to
the next. So the borders’ areas are 3 numbers in arithmetic progression with difference
−8. Since they sum to 999, their average is 333, which is the middle number.
Consequently, the borders are 341, 333 and 325 cm2. If only the outer two are removed,
this would be 341 + 333 = 674 cm2,
hence (674).
Alternative 3
+
+
+
= 999 cm2
= 24 cm2
+
+
= 999 −24 = 975 cm2
=
=
= 975 ÷ 3
= 325 cm2
+
+
= 999 −325 = 674 cm2
hence (674).

29. Rodney builds increasingly large shapes out of unit rods. The smallest uses six rods and
each larger shape adds a layer of hexagons around the previous shape.
Starting with 2000 rods, Rodney builds a single shape like this, as large as possible. How
many rods will he have left over?
Alternative 1
Suppose the shape has N hexagons on each side. Consider first the horizontal rods,
counted in vertical columns. These columns go
(N + 1) + (N + 2) + · · · + (2N −1)
|
{z
}
N−1
+(2N) + (2N −1) + · · · + (N + 1))

which we rearrange by making pairs that add to 3N, like (N + 1) + (2N −1) = 3N,
(N + 2) + (2N −2) = 3N and so on, giving that the number of horizontal rods is
3N + 3N + · · · + 3N
|
{z
}
N−1
+(2N) = (N −1)(3N) + 2N = 3N 2 −N
There are two other directions, but these have the same number of rods, making a total
of 9N 2 −3N rods. Estimating, 2000 ≈9N 2 −3N ≈9N 2 so that
N ≈√2000 ÷ 9 ≈
√
225 = 15. So we test N near 15:
N
14

1722
1980
2256
So the greatest N could be is 15, and then there are 2000 −1980 = 20 rods left,
hence (20).
Alternative 2
In shape number n, let R ≈2000 be the number of rods and H the number of hexagons.
There are B = 6(2n −1) = 12n −6 rods around the boundary and R −B non-boundary
rods. Counting 6 rods around each hexagon counts every boundary rod once and every
non-boundary rod twice. So 6H = B + 2(R −B) = 2R −B = 2R −12n + 6 and then
R = 3H + 6n −3.
By considering concentric layers of hexagons,
H = 1 + 6 + 12 + · · · + 6(n −1)
= 1 + 6 ×

⇒R = 3H + 6n −3 = 3(3n2 −3n + 1) + 6n −3
= 9n2 −3n
Then 9n2 −3n −R = 0 where R ⩽2000. We solve this quadratic for n > 0:
9n2 −3n −R = 0
n = 1
18

= 1

This suggests that n = 15 is possible. Checking, the number of rods when n = 15 is
3n(3n −1) = 45 × 44 = 1980 rods, which is possible. The next shape has n = 16 and
requires 48 × 47 > 452 = 2025 rods, which is too many.
So the largest shape has 15 hexagons on each side. It requires 1980 rods and has
2000 −1980 = 44 rods left over,
hence (20).

30. Tim uses plug-in timers to automate his indoor plant lighting.

Call the timers A, B, C from front to back.
The lamp will turn on when timer A has received 9 hours of power, and is starting its
10th hour.
For this, timer B will have delivered 8 hours on its first rotation, then 1 hour on its
second rotation. That is, it will have completed one rotation, and be showing 10:00. This
means it has received 34 hours of power, and just be starting its 35th hour.
For timer C to deliver 34 hours of power, it will have completed 4 rotations, which will
deliver 32 hours of power, and then another 2 hours between when it shows 9:00 and
when it shows 11:00. Since C is receiving power continuously, this will be 4 days and 11
hours after the power was first switched on.

The time it takes for the lamp to turn on is 4 × 24 + 11 = 107 hours,
hence (107).
